title: N(6)-Methyladenine in eukaryotes.
author_string: Alderman, Myles H, Xiao, Andrew Z
journal_full: Cellular and molecular life sciences : CMLS
abstract: DNA modifications are a major form of epigenetic regulation that eukaryotic cells utilize in concert with histone modifications. While much work has been done elucidating the role of 5-methylcytosine over the past several decades, only recently has it been recognized that N(6)-methyladenine (N<sup>6</sup>-mA) is present in quantifiable and biologically active levels in the DNA of eukaryotic cells. Unlike prokaryotes which utilize N<sup>6</sup>-mA to recognize "self" from "foreign" DNA, eukaryotes have been found to use N<sup>6</sup>-mA in varying ways, from regulating transposable elements to gene regulation in response to hypoxia and stress. In this review, we examine the current state of the N<sup>6</sup>-mA in research field, and the current understanding of the biochemical mechanisms which deposit and remove N<sup>6</sup>-mA from the eukaryotic genome.
